coredata.dc:title: Synergistic integration of Au nanoparticles, Co-MOF and MWCNT as biosensors for sensitive detection of low-concentration nitrite
coredata.dc:creator: Lei, Hua
Zhu, Han
Sun, Shuhui
Zhu, Zhenfeng
Hao, Jiace
Lu, Shuanglong
Cai, Yurong
Zhang, Ming
Du, Mingliang
coredata.dc:description: Nitrite has been widely existed in food and natural environment systems. To protect the environmental safety and human health, non-enzymatic nitrite sensor with reasonable linear response range, low detection limit (LOD) and excellent storage stability are highly desirable. Herein, we designed a hierarchical structure including the multiwalled carbon nanotube (MWCNT) as substrates, Co-based metal-organic framework (Co-MOF) and high-dispersed small Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) (denoted as AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT). Small Co-MOF nanoplates were firstly grown on the surface of MWCNT, and then provides abundant adsorption sites for catching Au ions. The Co-MOF plays a crucial role in controlling the growth and dispersion of AuNPs, and limits particle growth during the reduction process. Combining MWCNT with Co-MOF can significantly improve the electron transport capability of Co-MOF. On the other hand, small and uniform AuNPs distributed on Co-MOF can reduce the operating voltage and significantly enhance their catalytic activity for nitrite oxidation. Owing to the intriguing synergy between the components, the obtained nitrite sensor device exhibits a broad linear response range from 1 to 1000 µM and a LOD of 0.4 µM (S/N = 3) with a fast response balance (the response time ≈ 3.5 s) at a voltage of 0.72 V. Moreover, this sensor remains 94.5% of their initial response after 20 days of storage at room temperature in air. These results suggested that the AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT nanocomposite has a good application prospect in the amperometric determination of nitrite.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MOF
Au nanoparticles
Electrochemical sensor
Nitrite

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of AuNPs/MWCNT, AuNPs/ Co-MOF and AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT nanocomposite

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology and composition characterization of AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT nanocomposites: (a, b) Low and high magnification TEM images, respectively. (c) HR-TEM images. Insets in Fig. 1c are the corresponding HRTEM images of AuNP and Co-MOF. (d) The corresponding STEM-EDX mapping images.

(a) XRD patterns of the synthesized AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT nanocomposite, AuNPs, MWCNT, Co-MOF and the corresponding simulation; XPS spectra of the synthesized Au/Co-MOF/MWCNT nanocomposite: (b) Survey spectrum (c) Co 2p, and (d) Au 4f.

(a) EIS spectra of the GCE, MWCNT/GCE, Co-MOF/GCE, AuNPs/Co-MOF/GCE, Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE, and AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE in 5 mM K3Fe(CN)6/K4Fe(CN)6 solution containing 0.1 M KCl with frequency between 100 kHz and 0.01 Hz, frequency number of 50 and an amplitude of 0.2 V. Inset in Fig. 3a is the enlarger range of MWCNT. (b) CVs for GCE, MWCNT/GCE, Co-MOF/GCE, AuNPs/Co-MOF/GCE, Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE, and AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE in 0.1 mM PBS (pH=3) containing 2 mM nitrite at scan rate 20 mV s−1.

(a) Cyclic voltammetric curves of the AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE in the presence of a different concentration of nitrite (0–10 mM) in 0.1 M PBS (pH=3) at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1. (b) The calibration curve of the anodic peak current versus concentration. (c) Cyclic voltammetric curves of the AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE in the presence 2 mM nitrite at 10–100 mV s−1 scan rate in 0.1M PBS (pH=3). (d) The calibration curve of the anodic peak current versus the square root of the scan rate.

(a) Amperometric response of AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE obtained in 0.1 M PBS (pH=3) with the successive additions of different concentration of nitrite at the potential of 0.72 V. (b) The linear relationship between the anodic peak current and nitrite concentration. (c) The amperometric response of AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE for 200 µM nitrite during a long period time (1400s). (d) Reproducibility cyclic voltammetric curves of five AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH=3) containing 2 mM nitrite at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1. (e) Magnified view of cyclic voltammetric peak curves and histogram of current density for anodic peaks corresponding to 5 electrodes. (f) The amperometric responses of AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE obtained at the working potential of 0.72 V in 0.1 M PBS (pH=3) with 100 µM nitrite and different interferents (2 mM).
Schematic illustration of the synthesizing process for the AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT and the sensing mechanism of nitrite.

head.ce:title: Synergistic integration of Au nanoparticles, Co-MOF and MWCNT as biosensors for sensitive detection of low-concentration nitrite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids
School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aKey Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrite has been widely existed in food and natural environment systems. To protect the environmental safety and human health, non-enzymatic nitrite sensor with reasonable linear response range, low detection limit (LOD) and excellent storage stability are highly desirable. Herein, we designed a hierarchical structure including the multiwalled carbon nanotube (MWCNT) as substrates, Co-based metal-organic framework (Co-MOF) and high-dispersed small Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) (denoted as AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT). Small Co-MOF nanoplates were firstly grown on the surface of MWCNT, and then provides abundant adsorption sites for catching Au ions. The Co-MOF plays a crucial role in controlling the growth and dispersion of AuNPs, and limits particle growth during the reduction process. Combining MWCNT with Co-MOF can significantly improve the electron transport capability of Co-MOF. On the other hand, small and uniform AuNPs distributed on Co-MOF can reduce the operating voltage and significantly enhance their catalytic activity for nitrite oxidation. Owing to the intriguing synergy between the components, the obtained nitrite sensor device exhibits a broad linear response range from 1 to 1000 µM and a LOD of 0.4 µM (S/N = 3) with a fast response balance (the response time ≈ 3.5 s) at a voltage of 0.72 V. Moreover, this sensor remains 94.5% of their initial response after 20 days of storage at room temperature in air. These results suggested that the AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT nanocomposite has a good application prospect in the amperometric determination of nitrite.

ce:section.ce:para: With the progress of the times, human beings are sensitively aware of the importance of trace detection of pollutants that endanger the ecological environment and human health, especially the nitrite widely used in the field of the food industry and agricultural industry [1]. It is noted that excessive intake of nitrite may cause cancer [2]. The World Health Organization (WHO) sets the maximum content of nitrite in water to 3 mg L−1 and consequently, the accurate monitoring of nitrite in the environment is highly desirable [3]. To protect human health, developing appropriate treatment methods to detect the trace amounts of nitrite is an important topic. A series of analytical methods, such as spectrophotometry [4], chromatography [5], molecular absorption spectrometry [6], chemiluminescence [7], Raman spectrometry [8], spectrofluorimetry [9], have been developed for the determination of nitrite. These methods usually involve complex extraction and pretreatment processes, and only a few techniques can realize the real-time monitoring of trace nitrite. Recently, electrochemical technology has drawn more attention due to its high sensitivity, wide linear response range, and low detection limits. Electrochemical sensors include enzymatic and non-enzymatic sensors, and the enzymatic electrochemical sensors strongly depend on the external environment, restricting the practical electrochemical sensor applications [10]. Compared with enzyme-based sensors, the non-enzymatic sensors are seldom affected by the external environment and therefore developing stable, low detection limits and specificity non-enzymatic electrochemical sensors are highly desirable [11–13].

The working mechanism of the non-enzymatic sensor is that the material modified by the electrode surface causes the target to undergo a redox reaction to produce an electrical signal change [14–16]. Therefore, the catalytic oxidation activity of electrode materials is an important factor that determines the performance of non-enzymatic sensors. To enhance the catalytic oxidation activity, a series of transition metal hydroxides (oxides), noble metals and their nanocomposites are used in the field of non-enzymatic sensors [17–20]. The transition metal hydroxide has a large electrochemically active area and a porous two-dimensional nanosheet structure, so it exhibits high sensitivity and superior stability in sensing characteristics [21,22]. Noble metal nanomaterials have excellent catalytic activity, which can greatly improve the reaction efficiency, and have superior sensing performance under a low operating voltage window [23–25]. Although the simple transition metal hydroxide shows good sensing performance, its operating voltage is relatively higher. The noble metal nanomaterials can effectively reduce the operating voltage of the sensor, and however, it is easy to combine with the reaction intermediates and cause the catalyst poisoning [26,27]. Combining noble metal nanoparticles with transition metal oxides or low-dimensional nanomaterials can effectively improve these shortcomings [28–30]. However, the size of the noble metal nanoparticle and the uniform distribution on the carrier needs to be resolved. In recent years, metal-organic frameworks are a crystalline hybrid material with large specific surface area, tunable porosity, and excellent chemical stability, which have attracted great attention in fields of energy conversion and storage [31,32]. Their steady porosity enables their outstanding advantage in confining the growth and distribution of noble metal nanoparticles (MNPs/MOF) [33–36]. Therefore, the MOFs could be an ideal host material to control the size and distribution of noble metal nanoparticles. However, Co-MOF is usually a large-area irregular 2D nanosheet structure with poor conductivity, which severely limits the efficiency of mass and electron transfer in the electrochemical sensing process of Co-MOF.

Herein, we reported a facile strategy for the design of hierarchical structure including the multiwalled carbon nanotube (MWCNT) as substrates, Co-based metal-organic framework (Co-MOF) and high-dispersed small Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) (AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT). The conductive MWCNT were used as substrates for the grown of small Co-MOF nanoplates. Then, the Co-MOF was used as polyporous carrier for catching the Au ions and providing limited space for the growth of small AuNPs. The introduction of Co-MOF can effectively confine the nanoparticle size and obtain the high dispersion. The integration among the AuNPs, Co-MOF and MWCNT can provide abundant catalytically active sites and further reduce the working voltage of nitrite oxidation. It should be noted that it is still a challenge for non-enzymatic electrochemical sensors to obtain superior sensing performance at relatively low operating voltages. This work reported AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT nitrite sensor exhibits a rational linear range (1–1000 µM), low detection limit of 0.4 µM, and excellent stability under the low operating voltage of 0.72 V.

Cobalt (II) acetate tetrahydrate (Co(CH3COO)2•4H2O), Terephthalic acid (1,4-BDC) and N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation, Multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, M.W. 8000) and Sodium citrate dihydrate (Na3C6H5O7•2H2O) were obtained from Macklin Biochemical. Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) trihydrate (HAuCl4•3H2O) from Shanghai Civi Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. Nafion (5%) from Aldrich. NaNO2, KNO3, NH4Cl, NiCl2, CaCl2, Na2SO4, NaAc•3H2O, CuCl2•2H2O, glucose were all bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

The morphologies and structures of the synthesized products were observed by a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2000). The line-scan energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) were recorded using a STEM (Tecnai G2 F30S-Twin). The chemical states and crystalline structure of the synthesized nanomaterials were examined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) in the 2θ range of 5–90°. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained on a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometry.

In a typical procedure, 0.06 mmol of CoAc2•4H2O (15 mg) was dissolved into 10 mL DMF solution (Solution A). Next, 0.06 mmol of 1,4-BDC (10 mg) was added into 10 mL DMF to form a homogenous solution (Solution B). Then, solution A was mixed with solution B under magnetic stirring and maintained for 12 h at 90 °C. The obtained Co-MOF were washed three times with DMF and ethanol three times with successive cycles of centrifugation and removal of the supernatant. Finally, the Co-MOF were dried at 60 °C for 12 h. Briefly, 20 mg sodium citrate and 20 mL deionized water were added into a round bottom flask, and it keeps stirred for 30 min at 60 °C. Then, 10 ml of chloroauric acid solution (0.4 mmol) was dropwise added to above the mixture and reacted for 1 h at 60 °C. The AuNPs was separated by centrifugation, washed severaltimes and finally dried.

In a typical preparation process, 0.012 mmol of CoAc2•4H2O (3 mg) was dissolved into10 mL of DMF (Solution A). 5 mg MWCNT, 0.012 mmol of 1,4- BDC (2 mg) and 4 mg of PVP were added into 10 mL of DMF to obtain a well-dispersed suspension B by ultrasonication for 90 min. Followed by pouring solution A into suspension B, the mixture was stirred for 12 h at 90 °C. The subsequent processing of the obtained Co-MOF/MWCNT nanocomposite is the same as that of the above Co-MOF nanosheets.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of AuNPs/MWCNT, AuNPs/ Co-MOF and AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: 5 mg MWCNT, 10 mg sodium citrate and 20 mL deionized water were added into a round bottom flask, and it keeps stirred for 30 min at 60 °C. Then, 5 ml of chloroauric acid solution (0.2 mmol) was dropwise added in above mixture and reacted for 1 h at 60 °C. After that, the Au/MWCNT precipitate was isolated by centrifugation and washed several times. Finally, the AuNPs/MWCNT were dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The AuNPs/ Co-MOF and AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT nanocomposite were prepared with the same procedure.

The glass carbon electrode (GCE, D = 3 mm) was carefully polished with 0.5 µm and 50 nm alumina slurries, respectively. Then, the GCE was rinsed and sonicated several times with distilled water and ethanol before use. 5 mg/mL as-synthesized nanomaterials were dispersed in a mixture containing 20 µL of Nafion (5 wt%) and 1 mL water and isopropyl alcohol mixture (v:v = 7:3) by sonication for 30 min. The suspension (5 µL) was dropped on the GCE and then dried at room temperature.

All electrochemical measurements were performed with a CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instruments Co., China). The whole system was carried out in a conventional three-electrode system. The prepared nanomaterials modified on GCE was used as the working electrode. A platinum electrode was used as the counter electrode and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was investigated in 5 mM K3Fe(CN)6/K4Fe(CN)6 solution containing 0.10 M KCl with the frequency between 100 kHz and 0.01 Hz, frequency number of 50 and an amplitude of 0.2 V. The cyclic voltammetric was scanned from 0.3 to 1.0 V in 0.1 mM PBS (pH=3). The amperometric response of the AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT modified electrode was measured in 0.1 M PBS (pH=3) at 0.72 V.

The synthesis process of the AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT nanocomposite was shown in Scheme 1. In a typical procedure, the Co-MOF were firstly grown on the MWCNT and then, the Au ions were confined in the Co-MOF. After the reduction, the small-sized and high-dispersed AuNPs were formed on Co-MOF modified MWCNT. The structure and morphology of the synthesized nanocomposites were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. S1, the Co-MOF synthesized in solution without MWCNTs exhibit irregular nanosheets morphology. Fig. S2 exhibit the AuNPs/MWCNT nanocomposites without Co-MOF nanosheets and there is only a few of AuNPs formed on MWCNTs due to the weak coupling between the MWCNT and AuNPs. Fig. S3 indicate that with the MWCNT as substrate, small and uniformly Co-MOF nanosheets formed throughout the whole MWCNT, suggesting the substrate effects for the growth of Co-MOF nanosheets.

The TEM images of AuNPs/Co-MOF indicated the AuNPs with sizes of 2–5 nm are uniformly distributed on the Co-MOF nanosheet (Fig. S4). As shown in Fig. 1 a and 1b, when the MWCNT were modified with Co-MOF nanosheets, small and uniformly AuNPs were densely distributed on Co-MOF/MWCNT. By comparing the TEM images of AuNPs/MWCNT (Fig. S2), AuNPs/Co-MOF (Fig. S4) and AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT (Fig. 1c), it can be inferred that the cavity of Co-MOF can adsorb Au ions and limit the growth of AuNPs during the reduction process [37–39].These comparisons confirm that Co-MOF nanosheets could be an ideal host material to control the size and distribution of AuNPs. The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) diagram of the AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT (Fig. 1c) exhibits the lattice fringe of 0.234 and 0.322 nm, corresponding to the (111) plane of Au and (110) plane of Co-MOF, respectively. The STEM-EDX mapping images of Au/Co-MOF/MWCNT nanocomposite (Fig. 1d) demonstrate that the Co and Au elements are uniformly dispersed on the surface of MWCNT. The Co element is ascribed to the Co-MOF nanosheets. The XRD patterns of the synthesized AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT nanocomposite, Co-MOF and the corresponding simulation, as shown in Fig. 2 a. The main diffraction peak position (8.9, 14.1, 15.9, 17.8, 27.4, 29.0, 30.7, 33.9° (2θ degree)) can match with the single-crystal data of [Co2(OH)2C8H4O4] (CCDC 153,067 contains the supplementary crystallographic data for this paper (These data are provided free of charge by The Cambridge Crystallographic Data centre), suggesting a successful synthesis of Co-MOF [38]. However, the diffraction peak intensity of the synthesized Co-MOF is slightly different from the simulation Co-MOF, which is attributed to the synthesized Co-MOF having low crystallinity. Compared with Co-MOF, the XRD patterns of AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT has obvious diffraction peaks of 2θ of 26°, 38.1°, 44.3°, 64.4°, 77.5°, and 81.7°. The diffraction peak at 26° is consistent with the (002) crystal plane of MWCNT. The diffraction peaks of 2θ of 38.1°, 44.3°, 64.4°, 77.5°, and 81.7° correspond to the (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222) crystal planes of AuNPs [40,41], respectively, as reported in JCPDS card No.04–0784. These results prove the successful synthesis of AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was further performed to probe the chemical compositions and valence state of the AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT nanocomposites. As shown in Fig. 2b, the XPS survey spectrum of the AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT nanocomposites indicates the presences of C, O, Co and Au elements.

The deconvoluted C 1s spectra clearly show the peaks at 284.8, 285.7, and 288.7 eV (Fig. S5a) correspond to CC, CO, and OCO functional groups, respectively. The O 1s XPS spectra (Fig. S5b) show the Co–O (531.27 eV) and OH (533.1 eV) peaks. The deconvolution of the Co 2p spectrum indicated four typical characteristic peaks that can be assigned to Co 2p3/2 (781.6 eV) and Co 2p1/2 (797.4 eV), and the peaks at 786.1 and 803.2 eV can be attributed to shake-up satellites, indicating the presence of Co2+ in the AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT nanocomposite (Fig. 2c) [42]. XPS spectra of Au 4f is shown in Fig. 2d, the peaks at 83.9 and 87.6 eV correspond to Au 4f5/2 and Au 4f7/2, respectively, confirming the formation of metallic Au [43]. XRD and XPS results both strongly indicate the successful synthesis of the AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT nanocomposites.

The electron transfer ability of the nanomaterial modified on the glass carbon electrode (GCE) surface is a crucial factor that determines the performance of the nitrite sensor. Therefore, the electrochemical properties of sample modified GCE were investigated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in 5 mM K3Fe(CN)6/K4Fe(CN)6 solution. As shown inset in Fig. 3 a, the MWCNT modified GCE exhibit the smallest resistance, indicating that the MWCNT has superior conductivity and can facilitate the electron transport of the electrode. The EIS spectrum of Co-MOF/MWCNT reveals an increase in electron-transfer resistance (Ret) relative to the MWCNT modified GCE (MWCNT/GCE) due to the poor electrical conductivity of Co-MOF. Besides, owing to the growth of small-sized and high-dispersed AuNPs on the Co-MOF/MWCNT, the impedance of AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE is smaller than Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE. The electron-transfer resistance of AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE is close to that of MWCNT modified GCE. The EIS results indicate that the surface modification of electrode could change the impedance with the modification of different nanomaterials and shows that the AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE nanomaterial is suitable for sensing.

The AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE was investigated by cyclic voltammetric (CV) to study the optimal electrolyte pH to detect nitrite in 0.1 M PBS with different pH values (2–8). Fig. S6 shows the anodic peak current reaches the maximum at pH=3, which is selected as the optimal electrolyte pH value of the subsequent tests. Fig. 3b shows the cyclic voltammetric curves for bare GCE, MWCNT/GCE, Co-MOF/GCE, AuNPs/Co-MOF/GCE, Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE, AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE at scan rate 20 mV s−1 in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 3) containing 2 mM nitrite. The MWCNT/GCE and Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE electrodes both have an oxidation peak of nitrate at 0.85 V. The oxidation peak current of Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE has a significant increase when compared with MWCNT/GCE. The MWCNT can only improve the conductivity of GCE. However, the MWCNT integrated with Co-MOF not only increases the electron transport property but also provides the active site for the oxidation of nitrite. Besides, the oxidation peak potential of AuNPs/Co-MOF/GCE has shifted to the left compared to the Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE. With the further integration of AuNPs on Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE, the oxidation current density of nitrite sharply increases, and the oxidation potential decreases to 0.75 V. On the other hand, the poor current response could be observed in the absence of nitrite (Fig. S7).

These results indicate that small-sized and high-dispersed Au NPs can effectively reduce the oxidation potential of nitrite. When compared with the bare GCE, the oxidation peak current density for AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE is four times higher than that of the bare electrode with a lower oxidation potential. Due to the synergistic effect among the AuNPs, Co-MOF and MWCNT, the AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT nanocomposite could be suitable as electrochemical sensor for the detection of nitrite. To investigate the feasibility of the nitrite sensor, the electrocatalytic performance is tested by using the cyclic voltammetric method at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1 with the nitrite concentrations from 0 to 10 mM. As shown in Fig. 4 a, with the increased nitrite concentration from 0 to 10 mM, the anodic peak current significantly increases, and the peak potential shifts to higher values. The correlation calibration chart displays an excellent linear relationship between anodic peak currents and nitrite concentration with an R 2 value of 0.999 (Fig. 4b). To further understand the sensing mechanism, the controlling step of the AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE electrode reaction is explored by the CVs at the scan rates ranged from 10 to 100 mV s−1 in 0.1 M PBS solution (pH=3) containing 2 mM nitrite (Fig. 4c). As illustrated in Fig. 4d, the current density of the anodic peak increases with the higher scan rates, and the oxidation potential also shift slightly to the higher values. The electrode process in the electrochemical reaction process consists of two continuous control steps: diffusion control and kinetic control. The slow control step determines the overall reaction process. When the peak current has a linear relationship with the scan rate's square root (ν1/2), the total reaction is controlled by diffusion; when the peak current has a linear relationship with the scan rate (ν), the total reaction is controlled by kinetics. In this article, the peak current has a linear relationship with the ν1/2, and its linear regression equation is y = 6.451x + 10.587 with R 2 of 0.996, which shows that the oxidation of nitrite is a diffusion-controlled electron transfer process [11,44]. The results suggest that the AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT nanocomposites display an excellent electrocatalytic performance for the amperometric determination of nitrite. In addition, the anodic peak potential (E pa) versus napierian logarithm of the scan rate (ln ν) exhibit a linear relationship, and the linear regression equation are obtained as E p (V) = 0.01935ln ν (V s−1) + 0.84227 with R 2 of 0.996 (Fig. S8). The anodic oxidation of nitrite on GCE surfaces is an irreversible electrochemical process, which can be described as Equation 1:
(1)
E pa
= A +
(
RT
2
(
1 − α
)
n α
F
)
ln
v
Where F is Faraday constant (F = 96,485 C/mol), α is the electrotransfer coefficient, and n α is the number of transfer electron. The number of nitrite anodic oxidation electrons was calculated as n α= 1.32, indicating that the oxidation of nitrite on the surface of the AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT nanocomposites is an irreversible electrochemical process [23,45]. The AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT nanocomposite is denoted as [B] in the following equation.
(2)
[ B ]
+
NO
2 −
↔
[
B ·
NO 2 −
]
(3)
[

]
→

+ N

−
(4)

O 2

2

(5)

−

During the electrochemical reaction, the
NO 2 −
in the solution will first be adsorbed on the [B] to form a complex
[
B ·
NO 2 −
]
shown in Eq. (2). Co-MOF derived from the surface of [B] has a small area regular 2D nanosheet structure, which facilitated the transport of
NO 2 −
across the electrode surface modified with the catalyst. Besides, the porous Co-MOF also provides a large number of
NO 2 −
adsorption sites. As shown in Eq. (3), the
NO 2 −
adsorbed on [B] is oxidized to NO2 and loses an electron under the promotion of high-dispersed small AuNPs at a lower voltage. These results indicate that the Eq. (3) is the rate-determining step for nitrite oxidation. Subsequently, NO2 quickly disproportionates with H2O to produce
NO 3 −
and
NO 2 −
(Eq. (4)), and the eventual product of
NO 2 −
anodic oxidation is
NO 3 −
(Eq. (5)).

To explore the influence of operating voltage on the ampere responses of the non-enzymatic sensors, the amperometric response of AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE obtained at operating voltages ranged from 0.68 to 0.74 V in 0.1 M PBS containing 200 µM nitrite were investigated (Fig. S8). The amperometric response increases obviously with the increased operating voltages range from 0.68 to 0.72 V. However, the amperometric response increases slightly at operating voltages between 0.72 and 0.74 V. Therefore, the 0.72 V is selected as the optimal operating voltage for amperometric response tests. Fig. 5
a depicts the amperage current of the AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE electrode at 0.72 V. With the continuous addition of nitrite solution in PBS solution, the amperometric current response increases in a stair-like shape (Fig. 5a). Besides, the gradient is steep and quickly reaches a stable state. The inset of Fig. 5a shows the concentration-response portion of 1–10 µM. It is observed that a stair-like i–t feedback (the response time <3 s, Fig. S10) can be obtained quickly even when a low concentration of nitrite is gradually added dropwise. As shown in Fig. 5b, the AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE electrode show excellent linearity in the nitrite concentration range of 1–100 µM and 100–1000 µM, respectively. The linear regression equation is obtained as y = 0.01641x+0.07831 with R2 of 0.999 and y = 0.01173x+0.63539 with R 2 of 0.999, respectively (y and x are the response current and the corresponding nitrite concentration, respectively). Additionally, the sensitivity of the nitrite sensor is calculated using the calibration curve slope to be 0.223 µA µM−1. The limit of detection (LOD) of the sensor is obtained to be 0.4 µM (S/N = 3), which is far below the threshold of nitrite specified by the World Health Organization. This work reported AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT nitrite sensor exhibits high sensitivity, broad linear range, and low detection limits for detection of nitrite under the low operating voltage of 0.72 V., and Table S1 shows the values compared with the similar materials.

Stability is also an essential factor in evaluating the timely detection of sensors. As depicted in Fig. 5c, the stability of the AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE was investigated with 200 µM of nitrite by using amperometric measurements during the 1200 s period. It is clearly shown that the amperometric response of the AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE is stable within 1200 s, and the final amperometric current value only decreases by 4%. To evaluate the storage stability of the nitrite sensor, 6 GCE modified with the AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE were prepared under the same conditions and their CV curves were performed in 0.1 M PBS (pH=3) containing 2 mM nitrite at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1. (We test the as-prepared AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE every 4 days.) As the storage time increased, the oxidation peak current decreased slightly and the current tends to stabilize from the 16th to 20th day (Fig. S11). Besides, the oxidation potential remains unchanged. After storage for 20 days at room temperature in air, the AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE remains 94.5% of its initial current, indicating the remarkable storage stability of the nitrite sensor.

As shown in Fig. 5d, five electrodes were prepared under the same conditions, and their cyclic voltammetric were investigated in 0.1 M PBS (pH=3) with the 2 mM nitrite at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1. From the partial enlargement of the corresponding cyclic voltammetric and histograms of peak current, it can be found that the oxidation peak current values of the five AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE are almost the same. The relative standard deviation is about 1.95% (Fig. 5e). The results suggested the excellent reproducibility of the nitrite sensor, which is attributed to the stable structures of AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT nanocomposites.

To investigate the anti-interference of AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE as a nitrite sensor, a series of common interfering species (i.e., KNO3, NH4Cl, CaCl2, CuCl2, Na2SO4, NiCl2, NaAc, glucose) were studied by amperometric measurements (Fig. 5f). As shown in Fig. 5f, the amperage current of the AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE increases sharply after the addition of 100 µM nitrite, and there is negligible changes in current with the addition of 2 mM interfering species to the 0.1 M PBS solution (pH=3). The result indicates that the AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE has good anti-interference ability as a nitrite sensor.

To evaluate the practical applicability of the nitrite sensor, the AuNPs/Co-MOF/MWCNT/GCE was used for the detection of real water samples. In Table S2, the initial tap water and mineral water exhibit no existence of nitrite. Whereas, the nitrite concentrations of 97.2 and 98.5 µM were obtained by amperometry with the addition of 100 µM nitrite to the tap water and mineral water, respectively. Besides, the nitrite sensors also show satisfactory recoveries in the range of 97.2–98.5%. These electrocatalytic performances suggested that the nitrite sensor can be used for the real sample detection and have a good application prospect in the amperometric determination of nitrite.

In summary, the Au/Co-MOF/MWCNT nanocomposite have been facilely synthesized and successfully applied to the detection of nitrite in the real samples. These small-sized and high-dispersed Au NPs distributed on Co-MOF can significantly enhance their catalytic activity for nitrite and reduce the operating voltage, and the MWCNT increases the electron transmission rate. Owing to the synergistic catalysis of each component, the nitrite sensor exhibits an excellent electrochemical sensing performance with a wide linear response range from 1 to 1000 µM, a low detection limit of 0.4 µM, and excellent selectivity under the voltage of 0.72 V. Thus, the development of nanocomposite based on 2D MOF nanosheets combined with the conductive carrier and noble metal nanoparticles has a significant application prospect.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This study is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (Grant nos. 51803077, 52073124), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Grant nos. BK20180627), Postdoctoral Science Foundation of China (2018M630517, 2019T120389), the MOE & SAFEA, 111 Project (B13025), the national first-class discipline program of Light Industry Technology and Engineering (LITE2018-19), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities.

Han Zhu and Mingliang Du supervised the research. Hua Lei and Han Zhu designed and engineered this work. Shuhui Sun, Zhenfeng Zhu and Jiace Hao performed the characterization experiments with support from Shuanglong Lu, Yurong Cai, and Ming Zhang. Hua Lei wrote the manuscript and Han Zhu and Mingliang Du revised the manuscript. All authors contributed to the general discussion.
Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2020.137375.
